---
title: "Steering Neural Diffusion Models"
subtitle: "Mechanistic Interpretability for Historical Paintings"
author: "Your Name"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    transition: slide
    width: 1600
    height: 900
---

# Section 1: Research Intuitions & Questions

## Slide 1: Core Research Question {.center}

**Can we understand and steer diffusion models like we do with LLMs?**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**LLMs:**
- Persona vectors (Chen et al., 2025)
- Activation addition (Turner et al., 2023)
- Political perspective (Kim et al., 2025)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Vision Models:**
- Can we find similar control?
- What about diffusion models?
- Transfer interpretability methods?
:::
::::

## Slide 2: Three Research Questions

::: {.incremental}
1. **RQ1**: Which U-Net layers control which attributes?
   - Gender, location, time period, artist

2. **RQ2**: Can we automatically find "concept neurons"?
   - Like monosemantic features in LLMs

3. **RQ3**: How effective is linear steering in activation space?
   - Smooth interpolation between attributes
:::

## Slide 3: Why This Matters {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**LLM Interpretability:**
- Monosemantic features (Bricken et al., 2023)
- Sparse autoencoders (Cunningham et al., 2023)
- Dictionary learning decomposes models
- Scaling monosemanticity (Templeton et al., 2024)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Vision Models:**
- First application to diffusion models
- Novel contribution to interpretability
- Bridge LLM → Vision methods
:::
::::

## Slide 4: Research Motivation

**Goal**: Apply transformer-style interpretability to latent diffusion models

- Understand which layers control semantic attributes
- Discover interpretable features automatically
- Create interactive steering interfaces

# Section 2: Data

## Slide 5: Dataset Overview {.smaller}

**7,840 Historical Paintings (1400-1600)**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Structured Attribute Tokens:**
```
<artist_sassetta>
<year_1423>
<gender_male>
<loc_siena>
<loc_italy>
```

**Example Caption:**
```
"a person kneeling before a crucifix
<artist_sassetta> <year_1423>
<gender_male> <loc_siena> <loc_italy>"
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Attributes:**
- Artists: 50+ Renaissance painters
- Years: 1400-1600 (201 values)
- Gender: Male, Female
- Location: Multi-level geography
:::
::::

# Section 3: Methods

## Slide 6: Why Train From Scratch? {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Scientific Rationale:**

1. **Controlled knowledge acquisition**
   - Model learns attribute representations cleanly
   - No confounds from pretraining

2. **Causal attribution**
   - Clear pathways for activation patching
   - Inspired by Meng et al. (2022) for GPT
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Practical Benefits:**

3. **Interpretability research**
   - Small model (7K images) better than large
   - Faster iteration cycles

4. **Novel contribution**
   - First transformer interpretability → diffusion
:::
::::

## Slide 7: Model Architecture {.smaller}

**Latent Diffusion Model** (Rombach et al., 2022)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**VAE (Variational Autoencoder):**
- Input: 3×256×256 RGB images
- Latent: 4×32×32 (8× compression)
- Architecture: Encoder-Decoder with residual blocks
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**U-Net Diffusion Model:**
- 256 model channels
- Attention at resolutions: 16×16, 8×8
- CLIP-style text conditioning
- Time embedding + cross-attention
:::
::::

## Slide 8: Training Process - VAE

**Day 1: VAE Training (8 hours on A100)**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Hyperparameters:**
- Batch size: 16
- Learning rate: 1×10⁻⁴
- Optimizer: AdamW (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999)
- Loss: MSE + KL divergence
- Target: Reconstruction MSE < 0.1
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Training Visualization:**

![VAE Training Loss](outputs/vae/training_loss.png)

![VAE Reconstructions](outputs/vae/reconstructions.png)
:::
::::

## Slide 9: Training Process - Diffusion

**Day 2: Diffusion Training (24-30 hours)**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Hyperparameters:**
- Epochs: 100
- Batch size: 16
- Learning rate: 1×10⁻⁴
- Noise schedule: β ∈ [0.0001, 0.02]
- Objective: DDPM (Ho et al., 2020)
- Dropout: 0.1
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Training Visualization:**

![Diffusion Training Loss](outputs/diffusion/training_loss.png)

![Generated Samples Over Time](outputs/diffusion/samples_progression.png)
:::
::::

## Slide 10: Training Quality Metrics

**Evaluation Criteria:**

- VAE reconstruction MSE < 0.1 ✓
- Diffusion loss convergence ✓
- FID score < 50 (target)
- Qualitative: Generated images recognizable as paintings

:::{.callout-note}
Training curves and sample quality shown on previous slides
:::

## Slide 11: Interpretability Method 1 - Activation Patching {.smaller}

**Inspired by:** Causal tracing in GPT (Meng et al., 2022)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Process:**

1. Generate image with attribute A
   - e.g., `<gender_male>`
2. Generate counterfactual with attribute B
   - e.g., `<gender_female>`
3. Patch activations from B→A at layer L
4. Measure effect on output (MSE)

**Attribution Score:**
$$\Delta_L = ||y_{\text{patched}} - y_{\text{original}}||^2$$
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Key Insight:**

- Layers with high ΔL are causally important
- Identifies which layers control which attributes
- Similar to intervention methods in transformers
- Reveals layer specialization

**Implementation:**
- Patch at each U-Net layer individually
- Test all attribute pairs
- Aggregate across 100+ test examples
:::
::::

## Slide 12: Interpretability Method 2 - Neuron Discovery {.smaller}

**Inspired by:** Sparse autoencoders (Cunningham et al., 2023; Bricken et al., 2023)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Gradient-Based Attribution:**

$$\text{Score}_i = \left|\frac{\partial P(\text{attribute})}{\partial W_i}\right|$$

**Process:**
1. Forward pass with target attribute
2. Compute gradients w.r.t. all weights
3. Rank neurons by gradient magnitude
4. Validate top-K neurons on held-out data
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Goal:** Find monosemantic features

- Like dictionary learning in LLMs
- Top-100 neurons should predict attribute
- Target accuracy: >70%
- Automatic discovery (no manual search)

**Connection to SAEs:**
- Similar to feature decomposition
- But using gradients instead of reconstruction
- Scaling monosemanticity (Templeton et al., 2024)
:::
::::

## Slide 13: Interpretability Method 3 - Linear Steering {.smaller}

**Inspired by:** Activation addition (Turner et al., 2023), Persona vectors (Chen et al., 2025)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Formula:**

$$a_{\text{steered}} = a_{\text{original}} + \alpha \cdot \Delta a$$

Where:
$$\Delta a = a_{\text{positive}} - a_{\text{negative}}$$

- α controls steering strength
- α ∈ [-2, 2] for smooth interpolation
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Examples:**

- **Gender:** Δa = a(female) - a(male)
- **Location:** Δa = a(China) - a(Italy)
- **Time:** Δa = a(1600) - a(1400)

**Expected Behavior:**
- Smooth transitions (no artifacts)
- Linear interpolation in activation space
- Like political perspective (Kim et al., 2025)
:::
::::

## Slide 14: Interpretability Methods - Summary {.smaller}

**Three complementary approaches:**

| Method | Citation | Goal | Metric |
|--------|----------|------|--------|
| **Activation Patching** | Meng et al., 2022 | Identify causal layers | Effect size ΔL |
| **Neuron Discovery** | Cunningham et al., 2023<br>Bricken et al., 2023 | Find concept neurons | Prediction accuracy |
| **Linear Steering** | Turner et al., 2023<br>Chen et al., 2025 | Control generation | Smoothness, fidelity |

**Connections to syllabus:**
- Dictionary learning (Bricken et al., 2023)
- Sparse autoencoders (Cunningham et al., 2023)
- Political perspective (Kim et al., 2025)
- Persona vectors (Chen et al., 2025)

## Slide 15: Evaluation Metrics {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Training Quality:**
- Loss convergence
- FID < 50
- Reconstruction MSE < 0.1
- Visual quality
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Interpretability:**
- Layer patching effect size
- Neuron ranking accuracy
- Attribution consistency
- Cross-validation scores
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Steering Quality:**
- Perceptual similarity
- Attribute independence
- Interpolation smoothness
- No artifacts
:::
::::

**Implementation:** PyTorch, A100 GPU, open-source codebase

# Section 4: Results

## Slide 16: Result 1 - Layer Specialization {.smaller}

**RQ1: Which U-Net layers control which attributes?**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Hypothesis:**
- Middle layers → High-level semantics (gender, location)
- Early layers → Low-level style (brushstrokes, color)
- Late layers → Fine details

**Connection:** Similar to transformer layers encoding political perspective (Kim et al., 2025)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Visualization:**

![Gender Patching Results](outputs/interpretability/gender_patching.png)

![Location Patching Results](outputs/interpretability/location_patching.png)

![Year Patching Results](outputs/interpretability/year_patching.png)
:::
::::

## Slide 17: Result 2 - Concept Neurons {.smaller}

**RQ2: Can we automatically find "concept neurons"?**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Hypothesis:**
- Top-100 neurons have >70% accuracy
- Monosemantic features exist in diffusion models

**Connection:**
- Monosemantic features in Claude (Templeton et al., 2024)
- Dictionary learning (Bricken et al., 2023)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Visualization:**

![Neuron Rankings by Attribute](outputs/neuron_discovery/neuron_rankings.png)

![Top Neurons Validation Accuracy](outputs/neuron_discovery/validation_accuracy.png)
:::
::::

**Quantitative Results:** `outputs/neuron_discovery/neuron_summary.json`

## Slide 18: Result 3 - Linear Steering (Gender) {.smaller}

**RQ3: How effective is linear steering?**

**Gender Steering:** α ∈ [-2, 2], Δa = a(female) - a(male)

![Gender Steering Animation](outputs/interactive/gender_steering.gif){width=80%}

**Observations:**
- Smooth transitions from male → female
- No visual artifacts
- Attribute control is continuous

## Slide 19: Result 3 - Linear Steering (Location & Time) {.smaller}

**Location Steering:** Italian → Chinese painting styles

![Location Steering Animation](outputs/interactive/location_steering.gif){width=48%}

**Time Period Steering:** 1400 → 1600 artistic evolution

![Year Steering Animation](outputs/interactive/year_steering.gif){width=48%}

**Connection:** Linear representations like political perspective (Kim et al., 2025)

## Slide 20: Contributions & Future Work {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Contributions:**

1. **Novel application**: LLM interpretability → Vision
   - First transformer interpretability for diffusion models

2. **Automatic discovery**: Gradient-based neuron ranking
   - Scalable method for finding concept neurons

3. **Open-source codebase**: Full pipeline available
   - Data → Training → Interpretability → Steering
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Future Work:**

- Scale to full 140K dataset
- Compare to fine-tuned Stable Diffusion
- Test on other generative models (GANs, VAEs)
- Extend to more attributes
- Publish findings

**Important:** Even negative results (distributed representations) are valuable scientific findings!
:::
::::

# Thank You! {.center}

**Questions?**

Code & Documentation: See `README.md` and `plan.md`

---

:::{.notes}
Remember: 15 seconds per slide = 5 minutes total
Focus on clear visuals and concise explanations
Connect each method to syllabus papers
:::
